title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: sumvar package provides simple easy use tools summarising continuous categorical data, inspired Stata’s “sum” “tab” commands. functions tidyverse/dplyr pipe-friendly return tibbles.

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: Simple one-line commands explore variables R users Pipe-friendly tidyverse integration Tabular summaries can stored tibbles used downstream analysis. first moved Stata R 5 years ago, main thing missed simplicity “sum” “tab” functions efficiently explore data. template code perform commands, introductory R books tutorials eg. https://r4ds.hadley.nz/data-tidy.html, takes typically 3-5 lines replicate functions R. couldn’t find package quite simply efficiently explore data. Sumvar fast easy use, brings variable summary functions R.

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: call dist_sum() explore continous variable. tibble output shows: number rows data, number missing, median, interquartile range (25th 75th centiles), mean, standard deviation, 95% confidence intervals using Wald method (normal approximation), minimum maximum values. Dist_sum() show density plot histogram single variable, grouped density plot grouping varialbe. can save output dist_sum tibble use estimates downstream analysis, eg. sum_df <- df %>% dist_sum(age, sex)
code: # Example data set.seed(123) df <- tibble::tibble(   age = rnorm(100, mean = 50, sd = 20),   sex = sample(c("male", "female"), 100, replace = TRUE)) %>%   dplyr::mutate(age = dplyr::if_else(sex == "male", age + 10, age))  # Call dist_sum df %>% dist_sum(age) #> # A tibble: 1 × 14 #>       n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd ci_lower ci_upper   min   max #>   <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1   100      0   55.6  44.0  68.1  56.9  18.2     53.3     60.5  13.8  101. #> # ℹ 3 more variables: n_outliers <int>, shapiro_p <dbl>, normal <lgl> df %>% dist_sum(age, sex) #> # A tibble: 2 × 17 #>   sex        n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd   min   max n_outliers #>   <chr>  <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <int> #> 1 female    49      0   52.5  41.1  65.6  54.7  17.6  16.3  93.7          0 #> 2 male      51      0   57.2  46.8  71.3  59.0  18.6  13.8 101.           0 #> # ℹ 6 more variables: shapiro_p <dbl>, ci_lower <dbl>, ci_upper <dbl>, #> #   normal <lgl>, p_ttest <dbl>, p_wilcox <dbl>

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: explore distribution dates, call dist_date() - similar dist_sum. can also grouped second grouping variable. single date, histogram shown; grouping variable also called, density plot shown.
code: df3 <- tibble::tibble(   dates = as.Date("2022-01-01") + rnorm(n=100, sd=50, mean=0),   group = sample(c("A", "B"), 100, TRUE)) %>%   dplyr::mutate(dt = dplyr::case_when(group == "A" ~ dates + 10, TRUE ~ dates))  df3 %>% dist_date(dates) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>       n n_miss min        p25        median     p75        max        #>   <int>  <int> <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     #> 1   100      0 2021-10-25 2021-11-26 2021-12-22 2022-01-28 2022-06-12 df3 %>% dist_date(dates, group) #> # A tibble: 2 × 8 #>   group     n n_miss min        p25        median     p75        max        #>   <chr> <int>  <int> <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     #> 1 A        43      0 2021-10-25 2021-11-25 2021-12-17 2022-01-16 2022-06-12 #> 2 B        57      0 2021-10-27 2021-12-01 2022-01-03 2022-02-07 2022-04-20

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: tab1() produces tibble showing distribution categorical variable illustrates using horizontal bar chart.
code: df2 <- tibble::tibble(   group = sample(LETTERS[1:3], 200, TRUE) )  df2 %>% tab1(group)

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: tab() creates cross-tabulation two categorical variables. default shows counts row percentages, row column totals.

what: Check for duplicate and missing data
title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: explore proportion duplicate values missing values variable, pass dup().  send whole database dup(), produce summary duplicates missingness whole database. Dup() illustrates stacked bar chart.
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:200, age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 50), digits=0)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing.  example_data %>% dup(age) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   Variable     n n_unique n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing #>   <chr>    <int>    <int>       <int>             <dbl>     <int> #> 1 age        200      116          69              37.3        15 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: percent_missing <dbl> example_data <- dplyr::tibble(age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 50), digits=0),                               sex = sample(c("Male", "Female"), 200, TRUE),                               favourite_colour = sample(c("Red", "Blue", "Purple"), 200, TRUE)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. example_data$sex[sample(1:200, size = 32)] <- NA  # Replace 32 values with missing.  dup(example_data) #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>   Variable             n n_unique n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing #>   <chr>            <int>    <int>       <int>             <dbl>     <int> #> 1 age                200      120          65              35.1        15 #> 2 sex                200        2         166              98.8        32 #> 3 favourite_colour   200        3         197              98.5         0 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: percent_missing <dbl>

previous_headings: Check for duplicate and missing data
title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: explorer() generates single HTML PDF report summarising variables data frame — continuous, date, categorical, duplicates, missing data — one step. run interactively, explorer() prompt confirm whether columns named id pid excluded summaries. report written working directory opened automatically browser.
code: explorer(example_data)                      # HTML report (default) explorer(example_data, format = "pdf")      # PDF report explorer(example_data, id_var = "id")       # exclude an identifier column

what: Summarise and Explore Continuous, Categorical and Date Variables
title: Summarise and Explore Continuous, Categorical and Date Variables
text: sumvar package quickly explores continuous, date categorical variables. sumvar brings ease simplicity sum tab commands Stata R.

title: Summarise and Explore Continuous, Categorical and Date Variables
text: Install CRAN: install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("sumvar") # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("alstockdale/sumvar")

title: Summarise and Explore Continuous, Categorical and Date Variables
text: dist_sum() — explore continuous variable; stratify grouping variable df %>% dist_sum(var, group). dist_date() — summarise date variables; usage dist_sum(). tab1() — frequency table single categorical variable: df %>% tab1(var). tab() — two-way cross-tabulation: df %>% tab(var1, var2). dup() — explore duplicates missing values across single variable entire data frame. explorer() — generate HTML PDF exploratory data analysis report whole data frame. dist_sum() tab() include options frequentist hypothesis tests. See function help pages details. functions accept %>% pipe tibble data frame return results tibble, outputs can saved manipulated:
code: summary <- df %>% dist_sum(var)

what: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
text: Summarises minimum, maximum, median, interquartile range date variable, optionally stratified grouping variable. Produces histogram (optionally) density plot.

title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
code: dist_date(data, var, by = NULL)

title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
text: data data frame tibble. var date variable summarise. Optional grouping variable.

title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
text: tibble summary statistics date variable.

title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
code: # Example ungrouped df <- tibble::tibble(   dt = as.Date("2020-01-01") + sample(0:1000, 100, TRUE) ) dist_date(df, dt)  #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>       n n_miss min        p25        median     p75        max        #>   <int>  <int> <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     #> 1   100      0 2020-01-12 2020-09-21 2021-07-10 2022-03-16 2022-09-24  # Example grouped df2 <- tibble::tibble(   dt = as.Date("2020-01-01") + sample(0:1000, 100, TRUE),   grp = sample(1:2, 100, TRUE) ) dist_date(df2, dt, grp)  #> # A tibble: 2 × 8 #>     grp     n n_miss min        p25        median     p75        max        #>   <int> <int>  <int> <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     #> 1     1    50      0 2020-01-13 2020-11-02 2021-04-20 2022-02-23 2022-09-24 #> 2     2    50      0 2020-01-04 2020-09-24 2021-05-11 2021-11-22 2022-08-29

what: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
text: Summarises median, interquartile range, mean, standard deviation, confidence intervals mean plots density histogram. Default option density plot histogram can overriden options "bee" "box". function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble.

title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
code: dist_one(data, ...)

title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
text: data data frame tibble ... variable like summarise Default, additional arguments: density plot histogram Add option "box" show boxplot violin plot eg. data %>% dist_one(var, box) Add option "bee" show beeswarm plot eg. data %>% dist_one(var, bee)

title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
text: tibble summary variable median, interquartile range, mean, SD, confidence intervals mean (using Z distribution), plots.

title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, age = rnorm(100, mean = 30, sd = 10)) dist_one(example_data, age) # Default, with histogram and density plot #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.  #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>       n median   p25   p75  mean    sd ci_lower ci_upper   min   max #>   <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1   100   30.9  26.4  36.2  30.7  10.5     28.6     32.8  3.88  57.6 dist_one(example_data, age, box) # Option to show a boxplot and violin plot  #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>       n median   p25   p75  mean    sd ci_lower ci_upper   min   max #>   <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1   100   30.9  26.4  36.2  30.7  10.5     28.6     32.8  3.88  57.6 dist_one(example_data, age, bee) # Option to show a beeswarm plot (using ggbeeswarm)  #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>       n median   p25   p75  mean    sd ci_lower ci_upper   min   max #>   <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1   100   30.9  26.4  36.2  30.7  10.5     28.6     32.8  3.88  57.6 summary <- dist_one(example_data, age) # Save summary statistics as a tibble. #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.  # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% dist_one(age)

title: Explore a continuous variable — dist_sum
text: Summarises continuous variable, returning tibble descriptive statistics plot. grouping variable supplied, results stratified group. two groups, t-test Wilcoxon rank-sum test reported. three groups, one-way ANOVA Kruskal-Wallis test reported.

title: Explore a continuous variable — dist_sum
code: dist_sum(data, var, by = NULL)

title: Explore a continuous variable — dist_sum
text: data data frame tibble var continuous variable summarise Optional grouping variable

title: Explore a continuous variable — dist_sum
text: tibble one row per group (one row ungrouped) containing following columns: n Number non-missing observations. n_miss Number missing (NA) values. median Median value. p25, p75 25th 75th percentiles (interquartile range boundaries). mean Arithmetic mean. sd Standard deviation. ci_lower, ci_upper Lower upper bounds 95% confidence interval mean. Uses t-distribution n < 30, Z-distribution n >= 30. min, max Minimum maximum observed values. n_outliers Count values 1.5 x IQR Q1 Q3 (Tukey fence method). shapiro_p P-value Shapiro-Wilk test normality. Returns NA n < 3 n > 5000 (outside valid range test). normal Logical. TRUE shapiro_p > 0.05, indicating significant departure normality 5% level. p_ttest Shown two groups compared. P-value independent samples t-test, testing whether means two groups differ. Assumes approximately normal distributions large samples. p-values reported first row ; remaining rows contain NA. p_wilcox Shown two groups compared. P-value Wilcoxon rank-sum test (Mann-Whitney U test), non-parametric alternative t-test. Preferred p_ttest data skewed, ordinal, contain outliers, compares ranks rather means makes distributional assumptions. p_anova Shown three groups compared. P-value one-way analysis variance (ANOVA) F-test, testing whether least one group mean differs others. Assumes approximately normal distributions equal variances across groups. p_kruskal Shown three groups compared. P-value Kruskal-Wallis test, non-parametric alternative one-way ANOVA. Preferred p_anova data skewed normality assumption met, compares rank distributions makes distributional assumptions.

title: Explore a continuous variable — dist_sum
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, age = rnorm(100, mean = 30, sd = 10),                               group = sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d"),                               size = 100, replace = TRUE)) dist_sum(example_data, age, group)  #> # A tibble: 4 × 17 #>   group     n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd   min   max n_outliers #>   <chr> <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <int> #> 1 a        34      0   35.8  28.2  41.4  33.4 11.6   3.49  54.6          2 #> 2 b        26      0   29.2  24.7  35.6  29.1  8.76  8.56  42.5          0 #> 3 c        18      0   28.6  21.4  34.3  28.9 11.9   7.82  48.6          0 #> 4 d        22      0   25.4  20.2  29.6  25.3 10.6  -7.10  48.9          2 #> # ℹ 6 more variables: shapiro_p <dbl>, ci_lower <dbl>, ci_upper <dbl>, #> #   normal <lgl>, p_anova <dbl>, p_kruskal <dbl> example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, age = rnorm(100, mean = 30, sd = 10),                              sex = sample(c("male", "female"),                              size = 100, replace = TRUE)) dist_sum(example_data, age, sex)  #> # A tibble: 2 × 17 #>   sex        n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd   min   max n_outliers #>   <chr>  <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <int> #> 1 female    56      0   30.7  19.4  35.6  29.0  9.86  7.94  49.4          0 #> 2 male      44      0   28.8  23.1  33.6  28.3  8.07 11.7   44.8          0 #> # ℹ 6 more variables: shapiro_p <dbl>, ci_lower <dbl>, ci_upper <dbl>, #> #   normal <lgl>, p_ttest <dbl>, p_wilcox <dbl> summary <- dist_sum(example_data, age, sex) # Save summary statistics as a tibble.

what: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
text: Provides integer value number duplicates found within variable function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% dup(variable)

title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
code: dup(data, var = NULL)

title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
text: data data frame tibble var variable assess

title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
text: tibble number percentage duplicate values found, number missing values (NA), together percentages.

title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:200, age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 50), digits=0)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. dup(example_data, age)  #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   Variable     n n_unique n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing #>   <chr>    <int>    <int>       <int>             <dbl>     <int> #> 1 age        200      118          67              36.2        15 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: percent_missing <dbl> # It is also possible to pass a whole database to dup and it will explore all variables. example_data <- dplyr::tibble(age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 50), digits=0),                               sex = sample(c("Male", "Female"), 200, TRUE),                               favourite_colour = sample(c("Red", "Blue", "Purple"), 200, TRUE)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. example_data$sex[sample(1:200, size = 32)] <- NA  # Replace 32 values with missing. dup(example_data)  #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>   Variable             n n_unique n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing #>   <chr>            <int>    <int>       <int>             <dbl>     <int> #> 1 age                200      117          68              36.8        15 #> 2 sex                200        2         166              98.8        32 #> 3 favourite_colour   200        3         197              98.5         0 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: percent_missing <dbl>

what: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
text: Analyses data frame tibble, summarising continuous, date categorical variables, missing data duplicate values, produces HTML PDF report.

title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
code: explorer(   data,   output_file = NULL,   format = c("html", "pdf"),   progress = TRUE,   id_var = NULL )

title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
text: data data frame tibble explore. output_file name output file. Default uses <df_name>_report.html .pdf format Output format, either "html" (default) "pdf". progress TRUE (default), show progress bar building report. id_var Character vector column names treat IDs (summarised).

title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
text: Outputs html PDF summary. Output PDF typically takes longer. PDF output, LaTeX distribution must installed. TinyTeX recommended. install, run:
code: install.packages("tinytex") tinytex::install_tinytex()

title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Build example data from mtcars with some factors and a date column:   cars_example <- mtcars %>%     dplyr::mutate(       across(c(vs, am, gear, carb, cyl), as.factor),       date_var = as.Date("2025-06-01") +         sample(-300:300, nrow(mtcars), replace = TRUE),       id = dplyr::row_number()     ) # To run explorer:   explorer(mtcars)                  # with progress bar   explorer(mtcars, progress = FALSE) # omit progress bar   explorer(mtcars, format = "pdf")   # PDF output   explorer(mtcars, format = "pdf", id_var = "id")   # Identify ID variable } # }

title: Count missing values — miss
text: Provides frequency percentage missing values. Counts NA, "" character variables (empty character variables) missing. function accepts input dplyr pipe %>% returns results tibble. variables specified, returns list missing values entire data frame/ tibble. eg. data %>% miss(variable1, variable2)

title: Count missing values — miss
code: miss(data, ..., dp = 1)

title: Count missing values — miss
text: data data frame tibble ... variable(s) assess missing data dp number decimal places percentages

title: Count missing values — miss
text: tibble number valid values (n_valid), missing values (n_missing), together percentages.

title: Count missing values — miss
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:200, age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 5), digits=0),                                           sex = sample(c("male", "female"),                                           size = 200, replace = TRUE),) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. example_data$sex[sample(1:200, size = 30)] <- ""  # Replace 30 values with empty character vectors. miss(example_data, id, age, sex, dp=1) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   variable total n_missing pc_missing #>   <chr>    <int>     <int> <chr>      #> 1 id         200         0 0.0        #> 2 age        200        15 7.5        #> 3 sex        200        30 15.0       # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% miss()

title: Pipe operator — %>%
text: See magrittr::%>% details.

title: Pipe operator — %>%
code: lhs %>% rhs

title: Pipe operator — %>%
text: lhs value magrittr placeholder. rhs function call using magrittr semantics.

title: Pipe operator — %>%
text: result calling rhs(lhs).

what: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorical variables — tab
title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorical variables — tab
text: Creates cross-tabulation two categorical variables row column percentages, row column totals, optional hypothesis tests. Prints formatted table console (similar Stata's tab command) column variable name displayed spanning header levels.

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorical variables — tab
code: tab(   data,   variable1,   variable2,   show = c("row", "col", "n"),   test = c("both", "chi", "exact", "none"),   totals = TRUE,   dp = 1 )

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorical variables — tab
text: data data frame tibble. variable1 row variable (first categorical variable). variable2 column variable (second categorical variable). show display cell: "row" (default) shows n (row%), "col" shows n (col%), "n" shows counts . test Hypothesis test(s) report: "" (default) runs chi-squared Fisher's exact; "chi" chi-squared ; "exact" Fisher's exact (falls back simulated p-value large tables); "none" suppress tests. totals TRUE (default), includes Total column (row totals) Total row (column totals). Set FALSE suppress . dp Number decimal places percentages. Default 1.

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorical variables — tab
text: wide-format tibble (invisibly) : First column: levels variable1 (plus "Total" totals = TRUE). level variable2: {level}_n (integer count) , show != "n", {level}_pct (numeric percentage). total_n: row totals (totals = TRUE). p_chi, p_fisher: p-values first row, NA elsewhere (test = ""; individual tests add test, statistic, p_value instead).

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorical variables — tab
Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: Summarises frequencies percentages categorical variable. function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% tab1(variable)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
code: tab1(data, variable, ..., dp = 1)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: data data frame tibble variable categorical variable like summarise ... used. Passing additional variables raises informative error suggesting tab() two-way tables. dp number decimal places percentages (default=1)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: tibble frequencies percentages

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, group = sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d"),                                                   size = 100, replace = TRUE)) example_data$group[sample(1:100, size = 10)] <- NA  # Replace 10 with missing tab1(example_data, group)  #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   Category Frequency Percent #>   <chr>        <int> <chr>   #> 1 a               27 27.0    #> 2 c               27 27.0    #> 3 b               20 20.0    #> 4 d               16 16.0    #> 5 NA              10 10.0    #> 6 Total          100 100.0   summary <- tab1(example_data, group) # Save summary statistics as a tibble.  #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   Category Frequency Percent #>   <chr>        <int> <chr>   #> 1 a               27 27.0    #> 2 c               27 27.0    #> 3 b               20 20.0    #> 4 d               16 16.0    #> 5 NA              10 10.0    #> 6 Total          100 100.0

title: Summarise a categorial variable — treemap
text: Creates treemap view categorical variable function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% treemap(variable)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — treemap
code: treemap(data, variable)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — treemap
text: data data frame tibble variable categorical variable like summarise

title: Summarise a categorial variable — treemap
text: treemap

title: Summarise a categorial variable — treemap
code: data <- mtcars data$gear <- as.factor(data$gear) treemap(data, gear)  # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% tab1(group)

title: sumvar 0.2.0
text: Added explorer() generate HTML PDF exploratory data analysis report entire data frame, including summaries continuous, date categorical variables, missing data, duplicates. Added dist_date() summarising visualising date variables, optionally stratified grouping variable. Redesigned tab() Stata-style formatted console output. Now supports row percentages, column percentages, counts (show argument), optional row/column totals, configurable decimal places, chi-squared Fisher’s exact hypothesis tests default. dist_sum() now returns n_outliers (IQR method), shapiro_p (Shapiro-Wilk p-value), normal (logical) ungrouped grouped summaries. dist_sum() now automatically uses t-distribution confidence intervals n < 30, Z-distribution n >= 30. tab() now runs chi-squared Fisher’s exact tests default (test = ""). tab() now prints note observations excluded due missing values. tab() tab1() now raise informative errors wrong number variables supplied. explorer() now prompts interactively exclude identifier columns (id, ids, pid, pids) summaries. explorer() now includes correlation heatmap section two numeric variables present.

title: sumvar 0.2.0
text: dup(): fixed inverted label positions stacked bar chart. dist_sum(): fixed incorrect test dispatch (ANOVA vs t-test) grouping variable contains missing values. explorer(): fixed id_var scope generated Rmd; fixed CLI progress bar token names. explorer(): correctly recovers data frame name called via %>% pipe.

title: sumvar 0.1.0
text: Initial release dist_sum(), tab1(), dup().
